trialDesign.fullName: Bariatric Surgery versus Intensive Medical Therapy for Diabetes — 5-Year Outcomes
trialDesign.overview: ## BACKGROUND Long-term results from randomized, controlled trials that compare medical therapy with surgical therapy in patients with type 2 diabetes are limited.
trialDesign.design: We assessed outcomes 5 years after 150 patients who had type 2 diabetes and a body-mass index index (BMI; the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 27 to 43 were randomly assigned to receive intensive medical therapy alone or intensive medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y g
trialDesign.population: patients who had type 2 diabetes and a body-mass index index (BMI; the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 27 to 43 were randomly assigned to receive intensive medical therapy alone or intensive medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy.
trialDesign.intervention: assigned to receive intensive medical therapy alone or intensive medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy.
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: primary end point was met by 2 of 38 patients (5%) who received medical therapy alone, as compared with 14 of 49 patients (29%) who underwent gastric bypass (unadjusted P=0.

headings.background: Long-term results from randomized, controlled trials that compare medical therapy with surgical therapy in patients with type 2 diabetes are limited.
headings.objective: Long-term results from randomized, controlled trials that compare medical therapy with surgical therapy in patients with type 2 diabetes are limited.
headings.design: We assessed outcomes 5 years after 150 patients who had type 2 diabetes and a body-mass index index (BMI; the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 27 to 43 were randomly assigned to receive intensive medical therapy alone or intensive medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y g
headings.population: patients who had type 2 diabetes and a body-mass index index (BMI; the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 27 to 43 were randomly assigned to receive intensive medical therapy alone or intensive medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy.
headings.intervention: assigned to receive intensive medical therapy alone or intensive medical therapy plus Roux-en-Y gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy.

outcomes.label: Primary outcome (extracted)
outcomes.definition: primary end point was met by 2 of 38 patients (5%) who received medical therapy alone, as compared with 14 of 49 patients (29%) who underwent gastric bypass (unadjusted P=0.
outcomes.interpretation: Five-year outcome data showed that, among patients with type 2 diabetes and a BMI of 27 to 43, bariatric surgery plus intensive medical therapy was more effective than intensive medical therapy alone in decreasing, or in some cases resolving, hyperglycemia. (Funded by Ethicon Endo-Surgery and others

trialSummary.conclusion: Five-year outcome data showed that, among patients with type 2 diabetes and a BMI of 27 to 43, bariatric surgery plus intensive medical therapy was more effective than intensive medical therapy alone in decreasing, or in some cases resolving, hyperglycemia. (Funded by Ethicon Endo-Surgery and others; STAMPEDE ClinicalTrials.gov number, NCT00432809.) items.question: How does the process described in reference 8 relate to STAMPEDE 5-Year?
items.answer: The rationale, design, and methods of the trial have been reported previously. The complete protocol was approved by the institutional review board at the Cleveland Clinic and is available with the full text of this article at NEJM.org. Briefly, the trial was a three-group, randomized, controlled, nonblinded, single-center study involving 150 obese patients who had type 2 diabetes, in which the effects of intensive medical therapy alone were compared with those of intensive medical therapy plus either gastric bypass or sleeve gastrectomy. Patients were randomly assigned in a 1:1:1 ratio to one of the three study groups, with stratification according to baseline use of insulin. Eligibility criteria included an age of 20 to 60 years, a glycated hemoglobin level of more than 7.0%, and a body-mass index (BMI; the weight in kilograms divided by the square of the height in meters) of 27 to 43. All the patients provided written informed consent.
items.reference: Ref 8: "The rationale, design, and methods of the trial have been reported previously. The complete protocol was approved by the institutional review board at the Cleve..." — Schauer PR, Kashyap SR, Wolski K, et al. Bariatric surgery versus intensive medical therapy in obese patients with diabe

items.question: How does the process described in reference 9 relate to STAMPEDE 5-Year?
items.answer: trolled trials showed metabolic improvements after gastric bypass that were similar to those of our trial but showed less consistent improvements after gastric banding than did our trial. Lower rates of nutritional complications were observed after gastric bypass, sleeve gastrectomy, and gastric banding in randomized, controlled trials than the rates observed after biliopancreatic diversion.
items.reference: Ref 9: "trolled trials showed metabolic improvements after gastric bypass that were similar to those of our trial but showed less consistent improvements after gastric ..." — Kashyap SR, Bhatt DL, Wolski K, et al. Metabolic effects of bariatric surgery in patients with moderate obesity and type

items.question: Why is the following clinically relevant in STAMPEDE 5-Year? Context: The primary outcome was a glycated hemoglobin level of 6.0% or less with or without the use of diabetes medications. Pre…
items.answer: The primary outcome was a glycated hemoglobin level of 6.0% or less with or without the use of diabetes medications. Prespecified secondary outcomes included measures of glycemic control, weight loss, blood pressure, lipid levels, renal function, ophthalmologic outcomes, medication use, adverse events, and quality of life (as evaluated with the use of the RAND 36-Item Health Survey). Methods for ophthalmologic evaluations are reported in the protocol. The strategy for all three groups was the adjustment of intensive medical therapy (every 3 months for 2 years and every 6 months thereafter) with the goal of achieving a glycated hemoglobin level of 6.0% or less, without unacceptable side effects associated with medical treatment. Patients in the surgical groups were instructed to take daily supplemental multivitamins, vitamin B , vitamin D, calcium, and iron
items.reference: Ref 10: "The primary outcome was a glycated hemoglobin level of 6.0% or less with or without the use of diabetes medications. items.question: How does the process described in reference 23 relate to STAMPEDE 5-Year?
items.answer: Data on changes in retinopathy scores and visual acuity from other randomized trials involving bariatric surgery are lacking. In our trial, no significant changes were observed at 2 years after randomization or in the current 5-year analysis (Table S6 in the Supplementary Appendix). In contrast, initial worsening of retinopathy was shown in the Diabetes Control and Complications Trial within the first year of intensive medical treatment. Our results should mitigate concerns that rapid glycemic improvement with surgery could worsen retinopathy.
items.reference: Ref 23: "Data on changes in retinopathy scores and visual acuity from other randomized trials involving bariatric surgery are lacking. In our trial, no significant chang..." — The Diabetes Control and Complications Trial Research Group. Early worsening of diabetic retinopathy in the Diabetes Con

items.question: Which finding is supported by reference 24 in STAMPEDE 5-Year?
items.explanation: Limitations of our study include an inadequate sample size and duration to detect differences in the incidence of cardiovascular and end-organ complications and to detect some differences in outcomes between the two surgical procedures. Despite the intent to continue intensive medical treatment in the control group throughout the study, a reduction in diabetes medication use was observed after 3 years. Plausible explanations for nonadherence to medication use and lifestyle counseling include economic deterrents, unacceptable side effects from the drugs, and behavioral maladaptation. A higher degree of adherence may have decreased the efficacy gap between medical therapy and surgery.
items.reference: Ref 24: "Limitations of our study include an inadequate sample size and duration to detect differences in the incidence of cardiovascular and end-organ complications and..." — Kolandaivelu K, Leiden BB, O'Gara PT, Bhatt DL. Non-adherence to cardiovascular medications. Eur Heart J 2014;35:3267-76
